blurb: Queues, pub/sub, Kafka, delivery guarantees, idempotency.

questions.q: At-least-once delivery. Why must consumers be idempotent?
choices: A message may be delivered multiple times (on retry / rebalance)
questions.explanation: At-least-once: producer retries on timeout; consumer may crash after processing but before ack. Either re-delivers. Consumer must tolerate duplicates — typically via dedup keys or idempotent operations.

questions.q: Kafka consumer group: 12 partitions, 4 consumers. How many partitions per consumer?
questions.explanation: Kafka assigns N partitions / M consumers = 3. Key: a partition goes to AT MOST one consumer per group. Scaling beyond partition count means idle consumers — increase partitions first.

choices: Writing to DB + publishing a message atomically
questions.explanation: Dual-write: without outbox, writing to DB + Kafka can partially succeed. Outbox: write the message to an outbox table in the same DB transaction; a separate process publishes from outbox. Atomicity preserved.

questions.q: Exactly-once semantics in Kafka. Which combo enables it?
questions.explanation: Kafka transactions (0.11+) + idempotent producer (dedup on retry) + transactional-aware consumer (read_committed) provide exactly-once for Kafka-to-Kafka pipelines. Across-system still needs app-level idempotency.

questions.q: Pub/sub vs queue semantics. If 3 subscribers follow topic X, how many see each published message?
questions.explanation: Pub/sub (fan-out): each subscriber gets every message. Queue (load-balanced): only one consumer per message. Kafka is effectively pub/sub (each consumer group is a separate topic tap).

questions.q: Dead-letter queue (DLQ): what triggers moving a message to DLQ?
questions.explanation: Message failed N (e.g., 5) consecutive times → move to DLQ for manual inspection. Without DLQ, a poison message loops forever and blocks the partition.

questions.q: What's the MAIN reason to partition a Kafka topic by entity_id?
choices: Preserve per-entity message ordering + parallelism across entities
questions.explanation: Same entity_id → same partition → same consumer thread → processed in order. Different entities → different partitions → parallel. Round-robin partitioning loses ordering.

questions.q: A Kafka consumer group with 6 consumers is processing events from 12 partitions. One consumer crashes. What happens during the rebalance?
choices: All consumers pause briefly while partitions are reassigned; the crashed consumer's 2 partitions are redistributed among the remaining 5
Only the crashed consumer's partitions stop
Kafka replays all messages from the beginning
questions.explanation: Kafka's cooperative/eager rebalance protocol reassigns the dead consumer's partitions. During rebalance, consumption pauses across the group (eager) or just for affected partitions (cooperative/incremental). The 2 orphaned partitions get assigned to surviving consumers. Any uncommitted offsets on the dead consumer mean those messages are reprocessed.

questions.q: A poison message (malformed JSON) keeps crashing your consumer. The consumer restarts, re-reads the same message, crashes again — infinite loop. The partition is stuck. What's the correct fix?
choices: Implement a dead-letter queue: after N failed processing attempts, move the message to a DLQ and advance the offset
questions.explanation: Without a DLQ, a single poison message blocks the entire partition forever. The consumer should track per-message retry counts. After N failures (e.g., 3-5), publish the message to a dead-letter topic, commit the offset, and continue. Operators inspect the DLQ asynchronously.

questions.q: Your order-processing service consumes from Kafka, writes to a PostgreSQL database, and then commits the Kafka offset. The service crashes AFTER the DB write but BEFORE the offset commit. What happens on restart?
choices: The order is processed a second time (duplicate) because Kafka re-delivers from the last committed offset
questions.explanation: This is the classic at-least-once delivery problem. The DB write succeeded but the offset wasn't committed, so Kafka re-delivers. The consumer must be idempotent: use a unique order_id as a dedup key (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) to safely handle the duplicate.

questions.q: Your Kafka consumer lag metric shows consumer group 'payments' is 500,000 messages behind on partition 7, while other partitions have near-zero lag. What's the most likely root cause?
choices: A slow or blocking message on partition 7 is causing head-of-line blocking in that consumer's processing thread
questions.explanation: Kafka delivers messages in order within a partition. If one message on partition 7 takes a long time to process (e.g., a slow external API call, retry loop, or resource contention), all subsequent messages on that partition queue up. Other partitions on different consumers are unaffected. Fix: add timeouts, async processing, or identify the slow message.

questions.q: You're migrating from RabbitMQ (queue semantics) to Kafka (log semantics). Your current design deletes messages after processing. A developer asks: 'How do we replay last week's events for a new analytics consumer?' What Kafka feature enables this?
choices: Consumer offset reset: new consumer group starts reading from any past offset (beginning, timestamp, or specific offset) since Kafka retains messages by retention policy, not consumption
questions.explanation: Kafka's log retention (time or size based) keeps messages regardless of consumption. Any new consumer group can seek to an arbitrary offset or timestamp and replay history. This is impossible with traditional queues that delete on ACK. It's why Kafka is ideal for event sourcing and adding new downstream consumers retroactively.
